steps.title: Notice the atypical traits
steps.content: The passage says ecologists have learned much about wild-bee declines from bumblebees because bumblebees are heavily studied. Fontaine then adds an important qualification: bumblebees have social behavior and dietary generalism, traits associated with greater resilience to certain environmental changes. That makes bumblebees unusual among wild-bee genera.

steps.title: Avoid overbroad conclusions
steps.content: Choice A overpredicts future population changes and treats generalist diets as a simple guarantee of lower risk. Choice B goes too far by saying researchers should not use bumblebees even for similar bees. Choice C invents a ranking between climate variation and habitat destruction that the passage does not support.

steps.title: State the cautious inference
steps.content: Because bumblebees may respond differently from many other wild bees, data from bumblebees should not automatically be generalized to all wild-bee declines. The logical conclusion is that researchers need caution when extrapolating from bumblebee evidence to other wild bees. The answer is D.
